title: Quantum Coherence Reshapes Thermodynamic Bounds for Thermal Machines
authors: Sergi Vidal, Alba Mayor-Fernandez, Rosa Lopez
abstract: Thermodynamic Uncertainty Relations (TURs) set universal bounds linking current fluctuations to entropy production in nonequilibrium steady states. Their multidimensional generalization (MTUR) introduces matrix inequalities connecting current covariances and mean values. We analyze these bounds in a paradigmatic quantum thermal device, a two-terminal conductor, operating as a heat engine, refrigerator, or heat pump. We show that classical performance limits on efficiency and coefficient of performance remain constrained by the TUR when finite power or heat flow from cold to hot reservoirs is maintained, even in regimes dominated by coherent transport. We further identify the conditions that optimize TUR and MTUR violations, demonstrating that cross-correlations can enhance the joint precision of charge and heat currents near the linear-response regime.
summary: This paper explores how thermodynamic uncertainty relations constrain the performance of a quantum dot thermal machine. It demonstrates that the machine selectively stabilizes the fluctuations of its thermodynamic input and that quantum cross-correlations play a vital role in optimizing joint charge and heat current precision near equilibrium.
main_problem: Investigating how Thermodynamic Uncertainty Relations (TUR) and their multidimensional generalization (MTUR) constrain the performance and precision of quantum thermal machines in different operational modes.
main_result: The study reveals a role reversal of precision where the input resource is stabilized (e.g., charge current in refrigerators, heat current in engines), and shows that cross-correlations between charge and heat fluctuations allow the system to approach the MTUR bound more tightly near equilibrium.
method: The authors use the Landauer-Büttiker scattering formalism and the Breit-Wigner transmission model to calculate currents and their full covariance matrix, evaluating saturation parameters for TUR and MTUR.
model_or_system: A two-terminal quantum dot (single-level conductor) coupled to two electronic reservoirs characterized by different temperatures and chemical potentials.
key_observables: Charge current, heat current, electrical work power, entropy production, efficiency, Coefficient of Performance (COP), and current-current correlations/noise.
important_parameters: Voltage bias (V), temperature bias (theta), tunneling coupling (Gamma), energy level (epsilon_d), and the linear-response vs. far-from-equilibrium regimes.
main_assumptions: Wide band limit approximation (constant tunneling coupling), steady-state operation, and symmetric electrical/thermal biases around average values.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device schematic and a phase diagram of operational modes; Figs 2-3 show colormaps of electrical and thermal current precision (TUR) across the bias plane; Fig 4 shows the joint MTUR saturation; Fig 5 shows efficiency and COP heatmaps.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the thermal machine model, defines the TUR/MTUR framework, analyzes single-current precision in different modes, extends the analysis to the multidimensional MTUR including cross-correlations, and concludes with a symmetry analysis of the device limits.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into the trade-offs between precision and thermodynamic performance in non-equilibrium quantum transport, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the stability of quantum heat engines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Schematic representation of the two-terminal co- herent thermal machine considered in this work. A single- level conductor with energy ϵd is tunnel coupled to left and right reservoirs characterized by chemical potentials µL, µR and temperatures TL, TR, respectively, and is driven out of equilibrium by a voltage bias V and a temperature bias θ that determine the operating mode (heat engine, refrigerator, or heat pump). b) Operating modes of the system in the (V , θ) plane with parameters: T0 = 1, ΓL,R/T0 = Γ/T0 = 0.5, ϵd/T0 = 1.

FIG. 2. Colormap of the TUR factor for the electric current in the left reservoir, F I TUR, as a function of the temperature bias and electric voltage difference between the terminals. For clarity, values of F I TUR larger than 105 are saturated and dis- played with the same color. Machine operation regimes are indicated. Parameters: ΓL,R/T0 = Γ/T0 = 0.5, ϵd/T0 = 0.5.

FIG. 3. Colormap of the heat current TUR factor (left reser- voir) F J TUR as a function of the temperature gradient and volt- age bias. For clarity, values of F J TUR larger than 105 are satu- rated and displayed with the same color. Machine operation regimes are indicated. Parameters: ΓL,R/T0 = Γ/T0 = 0.5, ϵd/T0 = 0.5.

FIG. 4. Colormap of the MTUR factor, FMTUR, for the elec- tric and heat currents in the left reservoir IL, JQ L , respectively, as a function of the temperature gradient and voltage bias between the two terminals. Machine operation regimes are indicated. For clarity, values of FMTUR smaller than 10−4

Fig. 3 presents the thermal counterpart of F I TUR shown in Fig. 2, namely the saturation parameter F J TUR for the heat current JQ L from the left reservoir. Remarkably, the qualitative behavior is inverted compared to Fig. 2. Now, in Fig. 3 the TUR is best saturated in the engine regime, while larger values of F J TUR are found when the dot op- erates as a refrigerator. In the engine domain, the region of small F J TUR indicates that the heat flow from the hot reservoir is close to the TUR equality limit. Thus, in engine mode, the thermal input JQ L is the current that is made as regular as possible given the entropy produc- tion. This is precisely the thermodynamic resource that fuels...
